q1.prompt: Influenza A virus is capable of causing major global pandemics through a process known as "antigenic shift," whereas the measles virus (a paramyxovirus) only undergoes minor "antigenic drift." Which genomic characteristic of Influenza A allows for the reassortment of genetic material that leads to this sudden shift?

options.label: Presence of a circular RNA genome
Possession of a segmented RNA genome
Integration into the host cell chromosome
Use of a diploid positive-sense RNA genome
Lack of a viral-encoded RNA polymerase

q2.prompt: During the maturation of the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), the precursor glycoprotein gp160 is cleaved into two functional subunits. Which of these resulting proteins is primarily responsible for the initial attachment of the virion to the host CD4 receptor?

q3.prompt: Acyclovir is a guanosine analog used to treat Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) infections. It acts as a potent inhibitor of viral DNA synthesis because it lacks a 3'-hydroxyl group on its side chain. What is the immediate biochemical consequence when this drug is incorporated into a growing viral DNA strand?

options.label: Inhibition of the viral protease enzyme
Prevention of viral uncoating in the cytoplasm
Immediate termination of the DNA chain
Blockade of viral entry at the cell membrane
Enhanced degradation of the viral mRNA

q4.prompt: A 25-year-old healthcare worker undergoes routine screening for Hepatitis B. The laboratory results show a positive test for Hepatitis B surface antibody (anti-HBs), but negative results for both Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) and antibody to Hepatitis B core antigen (anti-HBc). What is the correct interpretation of this serological profile?

options.label: Immunity resulting from a prior natural infection
Immunity resulting from Hepatitis B vaccination
Window period of an acute infection

q5.prompt: Rhinovirus is the most common cause of the "common cold" but, unlike other members of the Picornaviridae family such as Poliovirus or Hepatitis A, it does not cause gastrointestinal disease. Which property of Rhinovirus accounts for its inability to establish an infection in the digestive tract?

options.label: It possesses a helical rather than icosahedral capsid
It is acid-labile and destroyed by gastric pH
It is an enveloped virus susceptible to detergents
It requires a coreceptor found only in the lung

q6.prompt: In the typical clinical course of an untreated HIV infection, the virus utilizes specific host cell coreceptors to gain entry. Which shift in coreceptor tropism is commonly observed as the disease progresses from the early asymptomatic phase to late-stage AIDS?

q7.prompt: A patient with a severe, recurrent HSV-2 infection is treated with acyclovir but shows no clinical improvement. Laboratory testing confirms the presence of a viral strain with a mutation in the thymidine kinase (TK) gene. Which of the following drugs is the most appropriate alternative for this patient because its mechanism of action does not require phosphorylation by the viral TK?

q8.prompt: Amantadine and rimantadine are antiviral agents historically used for the treatment of Influenza A. These drugs interfere with the viral replication cycle by targeting the M2 protein. What specific step in the life cycle is inhibited by this interaction?

options.label: Attachment to host sialic acid receptors
Release of progeny virions from the cell membrane
Uncoating of the viral genome within the endosome
Cleavage of the viral polyprotein precursor
Integration of viral DNA into the host genome

q9.prompt: The Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) genome contains the pol structural gene, which is essential for the production of infectious progeny. Which set of viral enzymes is encoded by this specific gene?

q10.prompt: Parvovirus B19 is a small, non-enveloped DNA virus that can cause a transient aplastic crisis in patients with high red cell turnover. To initiate infection, the virus must bind to a specific receptor on the surface of erythroid precursor cells. Which molecule serves as this receptor?
